cards.definition: The reddish-brown foam layer on top of a fresh espresso shot, caused by trapped gas — a sign of freshness, though not flavorful on its own.
cards.analogy: The head on a freshly poured beer.
cards.why_it_matters: Explains why a shot's top layer looks the way it does — worth knowing so it isn't mistaken for a flavor indicator.
cards.cloze: ___ is the foam layer on top of espresso, caused by trapped gas from fresh coffee.

cards.definition: Using fine needles to break up clumps in the espresso puck before tamping, to prevent channeling and promote even extraction.
cards.analogy: Combing out tangles before braiding hair, so everything runs smooth and even.
cards.why_it_matters: A small habit that noticeably improves shot consistency if you're troubleshooting uneven espresso.
cards.cloze: ___ uses fine needles to break up clumps in the espresso puck and prevent channeling.

cards.definition: Uneven paths of water through the coffee bed during espresso extraction, causing simultaneous over- and under-extraction in the same shot.
cards.analogy: Water finding a crack in a dam instead of flowing evenly across it.
cards.why_it_matters: The usual culprit behind a shot that tastes both sour and bitter at once.
cards.cloze: ___ is uneven water flow through the coffee bed, causing inconsistent extraction.

cards.term: TDS / EY (Total Dissolved Solids / Extraction Yield)
cards.definition: Measurements taken with a refractometer to objectively quantify how strong a brew is and what percentage of the grounds ended up in the cup.
cards.analogy: A precise thermometer reading instead of guessing "it feels about right."
cards.why_it_matters: Turns "does this taste balanced" into a number you can actually track and repeat.
cards.cloze: ___ are refractometer measurements used to objectively quantify how a coffee was extracted.
